text:
I initiated a timestamping routine at the dining table to record participant timestamps. I stood facing the table, started the phone stopwatch, asked each person to mark a timestamp, passed the phone to Katrina → Alice → Tasha, retrieved and turned off the phone, and secured it away. Conclusion: each participant recorded a timestamp and the device was secured for the meeting.

I opened the morning meeting at the tripod to set objectives and prompt inspection. I held the small tripod, stated the meeting focus on last-day tasks, introduced items by touching a hard drive and asking its name, proposed installing the tripod as a backup, and took a quick photo while prompting that items be opened for inspection. Conclusion: meeting objectives were set and tripod proposed as backup while item inspection was prompted.

I coordinated hard-drive dismantling and assigned roles. I moved left to observe others disassemble four hard drives, confirmed “Yes, only 4,” acknowledged Lucia’s experience, designated Shure as mentor/tutor, and relayed Shure’s offer of power banks from supplies. Conclusion: dismantling proceeded with role recognition and resource guidance.

I gathered and relocated boxes and cables for storage. I picked up boxes, organized data cables, moved boxes onto shelves, opened/closed boxes as needed, and returned to the table to oversee packing. Conclusion: boxes and cables were consolidated and relocated for storage.

I sorted disposables and staged materials for keep/discard decisions. I held a black bag and tripod, instructed to keep one box and discard contents, removed liners, consolidated trash into a new bag, retrieved Alice’s box/liner, and placed staged trash into a box. Conclusion: trash was consolidated and keep/discard decisions enacted.

I set up the microphone and guided attention to presentations and the workstation. I steadied the microphone, explained its use, pointed to the left screen, invited the group into my bedroom/workstation, positioned the stool, and encouraged them to come in. Conclusion: microphone positioned and group directed to the desk area.

I introduced the workstation layout and labeled computers. I pointed to each computer, counted devices (emphasizing six main units), named machines (e.g., “L1”), stated “All your data is in here,” and acknowledged Shure’s remark. Conclusion: computers were mapped and data locations identified.

I explained data transfer and hard-drive/cable setup. I described three-hour transfers, showed where glasses plug into the hard drive, demonstrated the hard drive’s two cables (data and power), simulated a computer environment via the power source, fiddled cables, and closed my laptop to show the setup. Conclusion: transfer schedule and hard-drive roles were demonstrated for data ingest.

We discussed power-loss contingency and proceeded to installation. Shure asked about outages; I said “That’s hard to say,” Shure suggested USB backup power; I then instructed the group to start installing, repositioned the tripod to avoid glasses obstruction, followed Lucia’s alignment guidance while steadied parts, handed screws to Shure, noted tool characteristics (non-magnetic), confirmed fit constraints for a large part, prompted Tasha to fix hair obstructing camera, and executed final fixes (tightened a screw, added a strip/stick, checked visually). Conclusion: installation proceeded with alignment, fastening, fit adjustments, camera correction, and a final completion check.

We selected background music and had brief local-interest exchange. Shure offered to play music; preferences were shared (David Tao/R&B, Western/energetic), music began, I and others sang/danced briefly, and I confirmed Shure’s hometown as Changchun after asking. Conclusion: music played, preferences exchanged, and light social confirmation occurred.

I begin the packing by opening the first cardboard box and inspecting contents. I pick up and open the box, reach in, retrieve a data cable, tidy the cables, listen as Alice, Katrina and Lucia discuss electrician experience while Shure says “Ready to go in,” then return the cable to the box. Initial inspection is completed and the box is prepared for the next step.

I display and move the box along the assembly area to coordinate the next workstation. I pick up the box, hold it up for the group, look around, walk back carrying it, and hear Lucia and Shure note the next station links to the computer and needs a senior technician before I close and stage the box. The box is closed and staged while skill requirements for the next station are identified.

I perform small adjustments and test-fit parts at the workstation. I place a box on the bed, sit and stand, reach for two screws, cup and adjust parts of a Shure box, state “2,000,” then finish adjustments and remain positioned to observe further assembly. Adjustments are completed and I continue observing assembly activity.

I monitor others and direct placement into the box. I watch team members fix items, instruct “Yes, yes, put it in this box,” point at the box as the entrance/last step, and hold out my hand to let Shure speak while the team follows directions. The team places items into the designated box under my guidance.

I retrieve the box from Shure and finalize packing for that task. After Shure places items, I take the box with both hands, close and straighten it; Shure announces everyone can exit and thanks the group, prompting applause. The packing for that task is completed and the box is secured.

I continue handling and stacking boxes while the group discusses invites. I stack two boxes, carry them across the room, place them on the stool and bed, and hear suggestions to write invitees on the blackboard with Shure offering to write. Boxes are restacked and the team agrees to note invitees on the blackboard.

I reorganize the workspace and propose additional assembly. I move stacked boxes into the foyer, point at the blackboard, suggest assembling a shelf, pass Shure and pick up another box to reposition the work area. The workspace is reorganized and a plan to write invitees and consider shelf assembly is initiated.

I transport and position a box and prioritize writing over assembly. I carry a box to the left, rotate and place it, tell others “you can put these down,” agree with Shure to postpone assembly, and set the box in the corner. The box is secured in the corner and assembly is deferred in favor of writing.

I search for a pen and move between floors with a clapboard while coordinating entry. I ask repeatedly “Where's the pen?”, search the dining/bedroom, ascend and descend stairs holding a clapboard, respond to Lucia’s question about guests, enter through a door, then locate and place the pen on the table after Katrina and Shure comment. A writing implement is found and positioned for use.

I seat myself and engage in the invitation planning led by Shure. I place and sit on the stool, ask “What's the difference?” about type labels, observe Shure write the final-day list, prompt where to write names, confirm figure ownership and guest count (“4–6” then “nine to eleven”), suggest recruitment sources and incentives, note post-production concerns, and prepare to explain the finalized plan. Shure documents the plan on the board, staffing is framed at nine to eleven, outreach and incentive ideas are accepted, and I remain ready to relay the plan.

Shure opened the meeting and initiated facilitation while I observed and affirmed. He wrote "To start" on the whiteboard; I stayed seated, leaned forward, and nodded to his prompts. The session start and my role as attentive participant were established.

Shure confirmed the questionnaire and activity readiness. He asked whether questionnaires were GPT-generated; Katrina confirmed hers was written by her and the group confirmed completion. Questionnaire status was clarified and the group moved into activity discussion.

Katrina presented a flower-based activity plan (seven-day cycle, pressing, acrylic embedding). She proposed vases, pressing petals for postcards, acrylic embedding and peripherals; I held and fiddled with the flower box, watched Shure, and gestured as materials/methods were described. The plan's purpose and execution steps were defined.

I received and prepared delivered flowers for demonstration. I carried a cardboard box from SF Express (Yunnan) to the dining table, straightened and unpacked items, and moved between rooms to discuss sourcing and timing. Materials were positioned and unpacked for the upcoming demo.

We organized a brief showcase and confirmed tools. I proposed “showcase a bit first,” located scissors with Shure’s direction, spoke with Shure, then sat to observe while Katrina noted carnation availability. Tool availability and a short demo were agreed.

Participants demoed materials and began cleanup while I monitored. Katrina opened the box, others packed, Lucy and Alice packed their things, and I tidied and oversaw materials. The demonstration concluded and the space was prepared for closure.

Final confirmations, phone checks, door and camera setup concluded the session. Shure approached as I checked my phone; Alice asked about peonies, Katrina confirmed; I closed the door with my left hand and tightened the camera screws until secure. Door and camera were secured.

Flower care timelines and types were established. Katrina and Tasha specified bloom and pressing timelines (1–3 days bloom, 2–3 days pressing), discussed nutrients and possible flower types and packages. Bloom, pressing timelines and intended petal use were confirmed.

Activity format and craft logistics were decided with my input. The group agreed six arrangements, vases bought; I proposed postcards, acrylic frames and bookmarks and outdoor leaf collection; Katrina confirmed a 1–3 day window and display plan. Format, materials and timeline were set.

I oversaw packing and took responsibility for the next box. I asked about other orders, told people to continue chatting, volunteered “I'll handle this one,” repositioned the box and found veggie leaves. Responsibility for the next item was assigned and acted on.

Lucia presented DIYs and filming logistics while I observed. She proposed jellyfish lamps and cardboard dog options, discussed kits and hot-glue alternatives; Shure confirmed cardboard availability and the group agreed to use ready-made scripts for filming due to time. DIYs, materials and filming approach were clarified.

I inspected another box, handled papers, and coordinated seed-paper sprouting. I opened the box, removed sheets, handed a paper to Tasha; she explained seed-paper (coffee-ground pulp with sunflower seeds) and petri-dish sprouting; I checked my phone and reported a rejection. Papers, seed-paper method, and next actions were noted.

I presented my seed-paper designs to the group while holding pieces of paper and watching Tasha place a sheet on the table: I stated they can sprout in about five days, explained I made five kinds (two simple, earth-related), confirmed they were my originals when Katrina asked, and repeatedly identified them as "seed paper" and eco-friendly and fairly sturdy. Tasha placed a sheet and the group observed as I described benefits and quantity. Recap: I conveyed the nature (seed paper), count (five kinds, two simple), sprout time (~5 days), and key benefits (eco-friendly, aesthetic, sturdy).

I described simple production and presentation methods for the designs and a seed card: I advised others to "just find some logos," use logos or drawings, "just write a word and frame it," referred to a "seed card," lightly applauded, and declined to show one item ("Oh no, I'm not showing you"). I summarized practical, minimal preparation steps for presentation. Recap: I provided concise, simple instructions for preparing designs/seed cards while keeping one item private.

I introduced a wildlife puzzle and outlined its basic concept: I said it was a big round base with many animal pieces and relatively difficult, then watched Alice and others as people responded and began packing up. Alice then explained the puzzle objective—fitting pieces to evoke "we are on Earth," noted seams/variable fits, proposed daffodil bulbs as prizes, offered to buy high-quality bulbs, and cited her experience growing them. Recap: I added the puzzle activity; Alice framed objectives, difficulty, and prize logistics.

The group moved to coordinate activities and turns: Shure counted four activities and asked whose turn it was, Alice volunteered to run the wildlife puzzle, and Katrina mentioned Haidilao and a received strawberry-planting kit. I observed and acknowledged the assignments. Recap: Four activities identified, Alice assigned wildlife puzzle, others noted personal kits/plans.

We discussed planting options and a Haidilao promotional kit: Katrina described the kit tied to Chibi Maruko-chan orders, Tasha suggested pea shoots, Katrina noted buying a plant would take a month to sprout, and others confirmed kits exist in dorms. I signaled agreement and proposed "let’s go plant trees by the sea," while remaining mostly observational through gestures. Recap: Group weighed kit convenience versus buy-and-wait; I proposed a planting location.

Shure proposed a second-hand auction and suitable cozy venues (small bars/coffee shops, example "Jumping Sea") and outlined mechanics: prepare short PPTs, present item stories, evaluate to award coins, then use coins to bid on items. Katrina summarized as "tell stories, earn coins, and buy items," and Shure linked it to environmental protection. Recap: Auction concept, method (PPT+stories+coins+auction), and purpose (reuse/environment) established.

The group began preparing materials while I handled a paper and briefly addressed Tasha: I looked at my phone, stood, held a paper with both hands, nodded, clasped my hands and apologized to Tasha, continued talking, then put the paper down and moved between bedroom and outside while watching others pack; Shure explained presenters would state a suggested starting bid. Recap: Preparations (papers/PPTs) underway, with my brief interaction with Tasha and attention to bidding procedure.

Shure explained auction proceeds would circulate as currency usable at other auctions or to buy remaining items; Alice and Katrina framed this as currency exchange/economic building, and Lucia proposed adding platform handicrafts and small-shop items, which Shure confirmed allowed. I observed and nodded as the inclusion and circulation concept was accepted. Recap: Auction funds to circulate and platform/shops/handicrafts can participate.

Members identified items and moved to finalize logistics: Tasha announced an item for auction, Shure asked for thoughts, and Katrina suggested inviting "him" on that day; the team planned attendee entertainment and services—use school lawn, provide drinks/snacks/afternoon tea, Shure to make coffee and buy skewers, grilling chosen due to knife considerations. I asked about lunch, said afternoon tea/sweets would suffice depending on headcount, and continued observing. Recap: Items, invitations, drinks/snacks, grilling/skewers, and my lunch/tea input finalized.

The group set timing and contingency plans and reviewed supplies: Katrina and others agreed on an afternoon start (~2–3 PM) adjustable into evening; ice options were to ask a café or freeze molds overnight (distilled/boiled water recommended), and schedule slots for auction and activities were to be allocated. I used my phone to type timing and proposed a simple dance performance (suggested Choiszt/TikTok style) while observing consensus toward dancing. Recap: Afternoon start (~2–3 PM), weather and timing flexible, ice and supply options recorded, and a simple dance segment chosen.

Shure proposed keeping the auction structure with short turns and the group agreed to preserve the auction as the core while limiting speaking time. Shure opened with “Listen to me” and suggested ~nine people ~5 minutes each; I compared to yesterday’s two-hour event, estimated ~1.5 hours, agreed when Shure recommended short stories, nodded and remained seated observing. Conclusion: auction remains central with capped individual times to keep the event short.

The team debated buying coins versus earning them and required storytelling before distribution. Tasha proposed buying real coins and noted entrants receiving coins; Katrina insisted attendees tell a story before receiving coins while Shure said coins could also be earned and auction remains central; I leaned back, conversed with Tasha, gestured with my left hand. Conclusion: procurement vs earning weighed and storytelling-before-distribution was raised.

The group defined multiple coin-earning mini-games to combine with self-introductions. Lucia suggested handing out coins and dice/Easter-egg hunt; Katrina proposed self-introductions with reward/punishment; Alice specified first=5 second=3; Shure suggested quizzes and puzzles; I nodded, used my phone, placed it on the table and watched. Conclusion: combine self-intros, quizzes/puzzles, hidden/handed coins.

Event date and promotion sequencing were tentatively set with promotion after earning coins. Lucia referenced late-month/Earth Day; participants mentioned 22nd and I said “I’ll go with the twenty-first”; Katrina said attendees must earn currency first and then promote, Shure cautioned about risk. Conclusion: tentative date 21st and sequence: earn coins then promotion.

I proposed large speakers and outdoor music. I asked the group “So do we have those big speakers?” and said “We can get those” and “If we play outside, we can put on some music,” while nodding and watching. Conclusion: plan to obtain big speakers and play music outside.

I clarified table items and beverage authenticity. I identified “The vase,” asked “Is this real liquor or fake?” received “Fake,” noted “This is my water bottle,” tapped the stool and observed. Conclusion: liquor confirmed fake, water bottle identified, vase to be removed.

I removed flowers and the group identified a thermos and its origin, agreeing on English wording. I stood, took flowers to the table despite warnings, sat; Lucia/Katrina/Shure noted the nearby object is a 304 stainless steel thermos, I said “Northeast China/Northeast Steel Plant,” Shure named Anshan Steel and suggested more international wording. Conclusion: flowers relocated and thermos origin noted; decide to consider English labeling.

I negotiated seating and swapped with Shure. I offered “Or sit here,” said “I'll take the small portion,” moved, swayed, and exchanged places with Shure while observing others. Conclusion: seating adjusted and I changed position with Shure.

Arrival window, pre-start activities, and whiteboard timeline were set and documented. I said “3 o'clock,” proposed 2–3 arrival with sign-in and flexible games; Shure asked about contamination of data and I replied it’s normal behavior while watching Shure write timeline items on the whiteboard. Conclusion: arrivals gradual before 3 with sign-in, pre-3 activities, main action at 3:00, and timeline recorded.

I moved to the shelf as Katrina outlined the 3:00 segment and session breakdown. While walking I confirmed items’ purposes as Katrina said 3:00 includes Q&A and introductions and later self-intros plus a knowledge quiz for ~40 minutes; I scanned the group, said “Brilliant,” and stood observing. Conclusion: session structure clarified (self-intro, quiz, introductions) and I monitored agreement.

I unpacked and distributed equipment, handled cables, and arranged power responsibilities. I crouched, opened boxes, handed cables to Alice, placed cables on the table, asked people to “Plug in one more,” identified a power bank owner (Katrina) and delegated plugging in. Conclusion: equipment unpacked, cables distributed, and power/charging responsibilities assigned.

Final logistics were agreed: timing adjustments, role divisions, on-camera content, bartender placement, lighting, and dessert sequencing. Group agreed to extend time if needed, Shure assigned parts (guest vs own), I proposed a camera-addressed mini-sequel about handmade crafts and trading, team placed mixing on a visible counter, I powered the main computer and noted four studio lights to set up later, and dessert/display prep was scheduled before promotion. Conclusion: roles, content segment, venue staging, lighting needs, and dessert timing finalized pending setup.

Budgeting for the dessert table was reviewed with DIY and professional cost ranges. Tasha gave estimates (800–1,000 then itemized to ~500–1,000 DIY), Alice echoed totals, Tasha noted basic equipment was bought but not items like a dehumidifier, and I vocalized objections ("Too expensive," "Over ten thousand") while nodding. Conclusion: cost estimates varied and DIY/basic purchases were recommended to reduce expense.

Tasha presented a visual layout of the dessert table and seating; the group approved it. I watched, nodded, said "Oh, OK," turned my head, Katrina and Lucia praised the display, and Tasha indicated a specific seat ("You can sit here later"). Conclusion: visual concept approved and a seating spot tied to the dessert table was identified.

Dessert consumption and portioning were discussed with attendees (students) expected to eat most servings. I observed, laughed, and nodded while Lucia noted teachers eat little and students grab servings; others joked about taking extras. Conclusion: portioning/serving should account for students consuming the majority.

Dessert preparation methods and timing were planned to allow make-ahead options. I asked about same-day vs day-before; Tasha explained refrigerated desserts and that Ching Po Leung can be pre-made and assembled; I suggested avoiding gelatine and adding taro balls/fruit, and Katrina/Lucia noted ready-made taro balls exist. Conclusion: group favored simpler, make-ahead assembly with ready-made components if needed.

Dinner format and timing were narrowed to simpler options around 5–5:30 PM. Shure proposed BBQ/pizza options (32" pizza mentioned), Katrina cautioned BBQ is tiring, and I confirmed timing ("So at 5:30," "A big pizza," "Yeah, 5"). Conclusion: plan leaned toward pizza at ~5–5:30 with program continuing into the evening.

Program order and timeline were set to start with introductions and reserve storytelling/competitive segments for later. I argued introductions first since attendees are unfamiliar; Katrina suggested evening familiarity for emotional segments and aimed for food/drinks around 5–6 PM; Alice and others prepared materials. Conclusion: start with introductions, target food/drinks 5–6 PM, hold storytelling/segments later.

Overall timeframe, recording rules, and hosting roles were finalized. Katrina proposed 2:30–8:00 with segments until ~6:30 then performances; I agreed and confirmed an explicit end time, we agreed "No recording during dinner," and Katrina assigned me as dessert host with product-introduction slots (3:40–4:20) and finale placement; I acknowledged. Conclusion: schedule and no-recording rule set, and I was assigned dessert/finish duties.

Entertainment, interstitial activities, and presentation tactics were planned. Participants proposed karaoke, singing/dancing, a short film, a data-transfer/wander period, and one-hour gaps between segments; I confirmed free-time windows and that we wouldn't film during pizza; Lucia proposed the cover-and-grill presentation for ordered grilled items, Shure endorsed it, and I approved. Conclusion: mixed performances, interactive period, and agreed food-presentation tactic with protected filming windows.

Prep staffing, equipment checks, recording issues, and materials planning were resolved. Tasha said two people handle morning prep, Katrina volunteered and recorded items; a dead power bank caused lost footage, Shure urged re-recording, I restarted devices and downplayed minor loss; I led jellyfish-light materials planning on laptop/notebook, proposing recycled lids vs. kits and noting simple ribbons/beads as options. Conclusion: prep volunteers, re-recording actions, and materials decisions were documented and accepted.

I narrowed sourcing options for activity materials to buying a packaged kit or procuring individual materials. While watching on my laptop I listened to Lucia suggest checking Taobao and offer to search online, and Katrina compared buying a package versus a materials kit; the group also agreed individuals would buy their own materials and discussed quantities (one–three) with Shure suggesting an IKEA trip. Conclusion: sourcing narrowed to kit vs. materials, Lucia tasked to check online and group to finalize quantities.

I presented GPT-generated event plans and proposed an eco-friendly Draw & Guess game. I explained grouping, target words (e.g., "solar energy," "zero waste"), and the rule of drawing only; Tasha and Shure raised abstraction/drawing difficulty, Lucia asked if descriptions were allowed, and Katrina noted evening timing preferred but limited afternoon availability. Conclusion: Draw & Guess rules and target words defined and timing constraints noted for scheduling.

I proposed a sandbox microplastics timed-foraging game to demonstrate hidden pollution. I described hiding tiny plastic particles in sand and having participants use tweezers and magnifying glasses to collect as many as possible within a set time; the group responded positively, calling it clever. Conclusion: microplastics activity specified with clear mechanics and learning objective.

I set and demonstrated timing and handled phone-based logistics. I activated and showed an alarm/timer on my phone to Alice, Tasha, Shure, Lucy, and Katrina, confirmed round decisions after Lucia noted the timer stopped, and used my phone intermittently for notes while others mentioned errands (Shure hairpins), media follow-up (Katrina), and a low phone battery (Alice). Conclusion: timer/alarm and phone-based tasks recorded to manage rounds and follow-ups.

We reached a tentative consensus and clarified performance classification and sound responsibilities. I confirmed proceeding after Shure’s prompt, I asserted the performance should be considered commercial and solicited experiences; Shure described awkward past gigs, Katrina noted need for speakers/mics, Shure volunteered to handle sound and band contributions were referenced. Conclusion: tentative plan agreed, commercial classification flagged for clarification, Shure assigned sound responsibility.

We planned room prep, adhesives, testing, rehearsal, and food logistics. I proposed tidying and decorations with an Earth Day theme; Katrina suggested stickers and no-trace adhesive hooks, I limited use to secure options to avoid damages, and Shure recommended testing equipment and trying desserts/barbecue with Lucia to calculate needs. Conclusion: pursue non-damaging decorations, test equipment, rehearse, and consider on-site barbecue.

I initiated immediate logistical checks and addressed minor cost items. I suggested checking deliveries, moved a stool, closed my laptop and adjusted a data cable while Alice confirmed a stand; earlier I commented skewer cost trivial and Shure gave a price estimate. Conclusion: immediate room and delivery inspections assigned and low-cost item purchases noted.

I led and participated in a sustained jigsaw-puzzle session with technical troubleshooting. I observed and directed piece placement, repeatedly picked up and set down pieces while others identified edges and debated checking the solution; I investigated a charger by picking it up and returning it, then checked Katrina’s and Alice’s phones to demonstrate a recording/privacy indicator mismatch and prompted a retry. Conclusion: puzzle assembly continued without resolution while charger and app recording discrepancies were examined.

I joined the table and adopted multiple preparatory postures. I said “Okay,” leaned forward, lay face down on the table, stood up, placed hands on the table, turned and lowered my head, and alternated leaning, standing, and bending as I prepared to engage. Summary: I repeatedly shifted posture while preparing to work.

I repeatedly handled and inspected puzzle pieces throughout the session. I picked up pieces many times, fiddled mainly with my right hand (occasionally my left), withdrew my hand or set pieces down, propped my left hand on the table, rested my head on my arm, and intermittently watched others while staying involved. Summary: I continuously engaged with pieces through repeated pick-ups, fiddling, withdrawals, and brief rests.

The group identified and debated imagery on pieces while I observed and confirmed. Others said lines like “Is this a toucan?”, Tasha: “Oh my god,” Shure: “A wild buffalo… it’s called a yak,” Lucia: “So abstract,” Alice: “It’s standing on grass;” I nodded, gave short confirmations (“Yes, this is amazing”), watched, and noted Shure asking an AI for a solution. Summary: the group labeled and questioned images while I provided brief verbal confirmation.

We discussed fitting strategy and attempted assembly despite imperfect matches. I asked “Will this be at the edge?”, tried fits using my left hand, moved pieces with my right hand, steadied the table, and tested placements as others noted approximate alignment and proposed actions (“Let’s put it back,” “randomly piece it together”). Summary: we actively searched edges and tested approximate fits.

I coordinated timing using my phone and set a recording plan. I took out and turned on my phone, showed stopwatch data to Alice and Lucia multiple times, said “Everyone’s been watching the time,” told Katrina and the group “To synchronize,” put the phone down, and said we would write the time on a frame; Katrina acknowledged. Summary: I presented stopwatch data and established synchronization and recording.

We debated BGM and automated assistance while working. I said “Play the BGM” / “Or continue the BGM,” Shure repeated BGM lines, Lucia asked about using AI to scan outlines, Alice noted speed and suggested retrying, and I said “Let’s try” to proceed with scanning ideas. Summary: we balanced audio choice with consideration of AI-assisted assembly.

I proposed switching to unboxing and used my phone to manage that transition. I said “Let’s unbox the packages,” asked where to unbox, opened and swiped my phone, walked forward holding it, operated a charger, picked up a data cable, and during this Katrina left to sort items; Shure expressed blood-pressure concern and disappeared from my phone. Summary: I initiated unboxing, used my phone and accessories, and observed departures/concerns.

I enter the bedroom to handle charging equipment. I pick up a data cable in my right hand, pick up the charger, note movement options (left still requires moving forward), walk into the bedroom, pick up the charger there, then put it down. Recap: I inspected and handled the charger and data cable while considering movement.

I connect the data cable to the computer and operate the laptop. I try to plug in the data cable, sit up, lie down, plug the cable into the computer, and sit to continue tinkering with the laptop. Recap: The data cable was positioned and connected and I worked on the laptop.

I inspect and manipulate a box and its cable amid group discussion. I pick up the box, handle it with one then both hands, pull out the data cable and plug it into the box, put the box down, later pick up my phone and put the box away; Lucia, Tasha, Alice, Katrina and others discuss reassembly, placement and power/disconnection issues. Recap: I handled the box and cable while the group focused on reassembly, placement and power problems.

I move, check the camera, handle the charger again, and respond to suggestions. I walk forward/back, turn right into the kitchen with my phone, pick up and fiddle with the charger, push garbage aside with my foot, look up at and turn on the camera while continuing to use my phone; Shure, Tasha and Alice offer break/setup suggestions. Recap: I moved between rooms, rechecked charging and camera, and coordinated with others’ remarks.

I observe and intermittently assist with jigsaw puzzles while holding my phone. I walk forward, watch Katrina and others assemble puzzles, glance at the camera, make verbal comments, clap, suggest trying a tail piece then withdraw my hand after Alice says it’s too big. Recap: I observed progress, vocalized judgments, and made one physical suggestion.

I check vase deliveries in the foyer and coordinate verification. I walk forward, speak with Katrina about how many vases she bought and whether all arrived, ask “How many did you see?” and tell others to switch sides while Katrina offers to check. Recap: I approached Katrina to confirm vase delivery and coordinate checking.

I inspect boxes on the kitchen floor. I enter with my head down, look at a box on the ground, step back, move forward again, fiddle with things using my feet, turn left and adjust my glasses while Alice and Lucia comment about possible contents and shapes. Recap: I closely examined a floor box while others discussed pieces.

I position equipment and hand a box to Shure while requesting tools. I steady a box with my right hand, ask to move the whiteboard onto it, hold and give the box to Shure, request scissors (Katrina says they’re outside) and confirm flower/water status; I pause while Katrina reacts. Recap: I transferred the box, directed placement and requested tools.

I offer puzzle help and circulate identifying pieces. I say I’ll piece a few together, extend/withdraw my hand asking if useful, follow Lucia’s turning advice, acknowledge misses, then confirm a fitting piece and move around several positions while responding to others. Recap: I actively engaged in assembly attempts and piece identification.

I go outside to the table and handle deliveries with a pole. I walk through foyer and yard to the table, pick up and swing a pole, confirm packages outside, state more arrived and a housekeeper will deliver remaining items, answer questions about specific items, then set the pole down. Recap: I navigated outside, handled a pole, and confirmed delivery arrangements.

I unwrap poles, select candidate pieces, and attempt to join rods while Katrina arrives to fasten bolts. I pick up poles, tear packaging, compare widths as Tasha/Lucia advise, rotate and reassemble rods, operate a lever repeatedly, wave/shake pieces to test fit, and hand poles to Katrina who begins screwing in bolts. Recap: Poles were unwrapped, best-fit pieces identified, joining attempts made, and bolts fastened.

We coordinate assembly roles, consult the manual, verify device status, and complete final attachments. I prompt comparisons, offer a computer, use my phone to coordinate, confirm a device is recording and charging (low battery), reference the manual per Shure, stack switches per Katrina’s instruction, adjust heights, operate the lever and attach a rod to the rack. Recap: Roles and sequence were agreed, recording/charging checked, and final height/attachment steps executed.

I began assembling the rack by repeatedly operating the lever and inserting rods to extend poles. I operated the lever by hand, extended a rod, shook my head side to side while working, inserted poles into the rack, stood up, disassembled and picked up poles, moved forward, looked at and pointed to a box, and installed another pole. Assembly was initiated and progressed through repeated lever operation and pole insertion.

I adjusted pole lengths and positions to fit the frame before finalizing placement. I inserted poles into the ladder/rack and operated the lever repeatedly while checking distance and alignment, asking “Is the distance not right?” and “Like this, is this okay?” Katrina and others said a piece “should be a bit longer” and that length could be adjusted; I looked up, lowered my head, and turned my head left and right to assess slant and parallelism. The team focused on correct distances and lengths prior to securing parts.

I addressed alignment and middle-piece issues while others searched for parts. I held the frame with both hands, operated the lever with both hands, suggested “Let’s secure one side” or flipping it over, and continued lever operation; Lucia and Tasha discussed a possibly missing or too-long middle piece, Shure told us to “lay it down” and searched for parts near me, and a case fell while I continued assembling. Work proceeded with repeated adjustments concurrent with a parts search to resolve middle-piece and alignment concerns.

I installed and secured multiple poles iteratively to stabilize the structure. I crouched to insert rods, operated poles, took a long pole and manipulated it, swayed my head while walking forward holding the support, stepped up to check poles, and installed additional poles as Shure noted stability needs (“so it doesn’t move around”) and suggested rough placement. Poles were incrementally installed and secured to reduce movement.

I and others held the rack for final adjustments and then paused. I stood holding the rack with both hands alongside others, looked up and down while operating the lever with both hands, asked “Are you guys done assembling?” and proposed “Let’s take a break first”; others agreed they were tired and I offered further help. The team transitioned from active assembly to a coordinated pause with offers of assistance.

While holding the assembled shelf/rack I discussed next steps for the space. I, still holding the shelf, asked about our room, said I might need to decorate a bit or that we could check things out, raised my head and spoke during these remarks. The immediate follow-up was to plan minor room/decor checks after the assembly break.

I monitored and displayed a timer for the group. I checked the timer on my phone (slid the screen, opened the timer page, said “Come on”) and positioned the device to monitor it; I directed Lucia and Tasha and asked the backseat to watch. The group focused on my phone, confirmed the timer had started (Lucia: “Ah, it’s started”; Shure: “It should be almost one”; Alice: “Yay!”), and we continued observing the phone together.

We arrived and began unloading from the car. The car stopped, I opened the door, checked my phone and told everyone we had a lot of stuff and to hold on; Katrina, Tasha, Lucia and Alice exited, the driver stood by, and we opened the trunk. People started removing luggage and supplies, several items were heavy and a heavy item spilled; I walked over (groaned about my head), Alice held an item while I took it, Alice and Tasha tidied fallen items, and the driver closed the trunk.

We left the car and walked toward home while redistributing loads. After saying “Let’s go” we walked past the car, turned at an intersection and proceeded; along the way we discussed route and posters, entered a narrow alley and felt uncertain of the way. I coordinated load swaps (one-for-one swap), asked two girls to take lighter items, and adjusted who carried which bags as we continued.

We corrected route and discussed the Xiaomi oven. We backtracked from a wrong road, turned onto a wider tree-lined street, entered a small alley where I suggested putting the Xiaomi in its bag, noted a small dog and light strips, and proceeded toward the courtyard.

I retrieved and entered the door password. I asked whether the door had a password, opened WeChat, scrolled chat history to find the saved code, said “press the 3 again” (Lucia referenced “925”), entered the password and confirmed the door unlocked.

I entered, collected fallen items and secured a hat. I walked into the courtyard, picked up things that had fallen (Alice identified a hat), placed the hat into a plastic bag, then closed the door and proceeded into the living-room area.

I preserved a desktop transfer, unpacked items, charged my phone, and directed fridge storage. I left a bedroom desktop powered to continue a data transfer and later checked it was still active; I unpacked purchases (placed food in kitchen/fridge, removed helmet, returned a pen upstairs to the living room corner, set my phone on a chair, connected a power bank and cable), then led perishables placement (instructed fridge vs freezer, confirmed freezer items, watched loading and closed the living-room door).

I assessed the refrigerator, removed items, noted it was small and crowded, and placed earlier leftovers on the dining table; Katrina pointed out no cling film and suggested discarding some items, which I agreed to. Conclusion: we decided to discard certain items, relocate goods (Katrina to move wine to the second floor), and prepare food on the first floor.

I tidied the dining table, noted six mangoes and a pineapple, and joined the group discussion: Lucia asked about eggs, Lucia and Tasha decided mangoes could be desserts or eaten tonight, Tasha confirmed the pineapple was intended for tonight and I noted I’d also bought one. Conclusion: fruit was allocated between tonight’s consumption and storage; egg/pineapple placement pending.

I checked supplies and opened my phone (Meituan, WeChat) to prepare a takeout order while the group inventoried milk, shrimp paste, multiple creams, blueberries, and eggs; Alice and Tasha directed placements and I put frozen items into the refrigerator, separating cooking ingredients from snacks. Conclusion: groceries were assigned storage and I prepared to place the order.

We confirmed storage: Katrina reiterated second-floor wine placement; Tasha/Alice placed eggs, milk, cream; Lucia suggested checking an unused fridge; the group gave rough supply estimates. Conclusion: immediate storage and consumption decisions set, fridge space limited, takeout planned.

I initiated the dinner-choice discussion, proposing multiple options (BBQ, rice bowls, Yoshinoya, salmon, grilled fish, snail noodles/luosifen, luosifen, etc.); Tasha advised listing choices, Lucia pressed for a decision but none was made. Conclusion: options noted, no final selection.

I handled incoming groceries and improved lighting: I picked up strawberries/blueberries and a hotpot seasoning packet, turned on the kitchen light to aid unpacking, and followed Tasha’s directions as items were placed. Conclusion: unpacking progressed under improved lighting.

I decided vegetables should go in the refrigerator, examined hotpot base and konjac, and observed Lucia/Tasha/Alice debate which items required refrigeration; some vegetables left out. Conclusion: refrigeration applied selectively.

I modified and washed a trash-can liner repurposed as a flower container, coordinated cleaning with Alice and Lucia, consolidated leftovers into a small trash bag, wiped plates, and scraped food into the bag with assistance. Conclusion: indoor and outdoor litter were collected and consolidated.

I retrieved outdoor items (hotpot base, seasonings, two sweet-potato bags, two partially consumed juices), brought the bottles inside, placed them on the table/step, and confirmed they should be consumed quickly. Conclusion: outside groceries and unfinished drinks were returned and staged for consumption.

I finalized tasks by writing the schedule on the blackboard, announcing completion, and confirming leftover trash bags would be saved for later disposal. Conclusion: schedule and immediate follow-ups confirmed; tidying session completed.

I cleared the dining table and prepared garbage for disposal. I wiped the table with paper, folded large boxes, collected extra trash and bottles into a bag, tied it closed at the front gate to prevent spilling while walking slowly because it was dark, then returned to the house entrance. Conclusion: the table was cleared and the secured garbage bag placed at the gate.

I re-entered and joined everyone at the dining table to continue cleanup and discussions. I opened the door, entered the foyer, closed it, positioned myself at the dining table, pushed my glasses down, inspected and cleaned oily plates with a wet wipe then tissue; Katrina, Lucia, and Alice debated dilution volumes (0.3 L/300 mL) and discussed using an unidentified water bottle (later identified as Tasha’s) to water plants; Katrina watered and arranged/pressed flowers while Alice instructed leaf removal, Lucia asked Siri to play music and I acknowledged. Conclusion: plates cleaned and table discussions plus flower-arranging proceeded without a finalized gift exchange.

I moved into the kitchen to test cleaning supplies and wash dishes. After Alice asked about dish soap and the group agreed to try kitchen paper, I walked in with a plate, said “Let’s use it,” discarded trash in the can, placed plates in the sink, washed them with water and wiped them, then disposed of remaining trash. Conclusion: dishes were tested and washed using agreed supplies.

The group realized takeout hadn’t been ordered and began selecting dinner. I opened my phone, browsed delivery options while commenting, stayed undecided; Tasha looked at my phone, initially picked pickled cabbage stew then selected braised potatoes from “Potato Hero,” I searched vendors and suggested the potato shop with shredded potatoes and a braised pork with potatoes rice bowl, Alice ordered boiled meat and noted “no cilantro.” Conclusion: potato shop chosen and individual ordering left open.

I went upstairs to power and monitor devices in the second-floor living room. I walked up, turned on the sofa computer and adjacent computer (waiting for slow boot), powered a floor computer, entered commands, inspected my computer data, glanced at the corner camera and counted devices while monitoring statuses. Conclusion: multiple machines were initialized and monitored.

I came downstairs to confirm tonight’s plan and observe menu planning. I asked Tasha whether there were events tonight; she and others confirmed cooking plus a 30-minute dessert period; Katrina recorded menu options on the blackboard (various meats and a veggie), I observed, prompted (“Want two kinds of meat”), watched Alice place a takeout order, then returned to my bedroom. Conclusion: activity and preliminary menu were clarified and noted on the board.

I checked and charged devices in my bedroom, then finalized cooking timing and meeting plans. I turned on lights, checked the desk computer, retrieved and charged two power banks with a data cable, closed the window, placed pocket items, monitored computer activity, then returned to the living room where Tasha proposed starting soup and listed dishes; I asked duration, stated “40 minutes,” agreed to an 8:30 meeting, and the group proceeded to start food preparation. Conclusion: devices were charged and the group agreed immediate cooking tasks, a 40-minute estimate, and an 8:30 meet-up.

I initiated dinner preparations after confirming the takeout and prompted the group to start. I said "Come on, let's start," Tasha agreed and referenced "The sacred handover ceremony," and I checked the time (a little past seven). The group concurred to begin dinner tasks on schedule.

We evaluated making soup and helper needs. I proposed "If we're making soup, we need a few people," Tasha requested a helper repeatedly, I alternately declined and offered self-help, and we confirmed no soup was ordered. Soup prep was proposed but cancelled due to ordering.

I checked attendees to decide if more food was needed. I planned to ask whether Xie Mingzhu would come and monitored if others would attend; we noted no rice had been ordered and that additional people would require more dishes/rice. Attendance checks tied directly to potential reordering.

I handled phone-based ordering, payment, and ring management. I reviewed the takeout on my phone, messaged attendees, indicated readiness to pay, removed and assigned someone to watch my ring, completed payment, and placed the takeout order. Payment and ordering were completed and ring handling was arranged.

Ingredients and a frozen item were staged and a device needed charging. Lucia placed ingredients on the table, Alice reported an item "frozen rock solid," I suggested charging the device and connecting it first, and Lucia agreed. Ingredients were staged and the charging task was assigned.

Utensils and knife responsibilities were assigned. Alice requested a basin, big knife, and pot; I said I'd get a big knife, walked to the table, and queried whether the current knife was sufficient while Katrina crouched nearby. Knife sourcing and prep responsibility were distributed; sharpness was discussed but no immediate cutting occurred.

I confirmed ingredient counts and paused. I verified we bought one bag of enoki mushrooms (not two), Alice agreed, outstanding tasks remained, and I sat to rest briefly. Counts were confirmed and work was momentarily paused.

I joined kitchen prep coordination with Alice and Tasha. I watched Alice present a cutting board and request help keeping items dry, observed Tasha note recipe steps (e.g., "add sugar three times"), search for an egg pricker, and note oven temperature while Alice tidied. Recipe tasks and utensil instructions were organized for execution.

I obtained sterile gloves and located century eggs. I asked "Where are my sterile gloves?", Tasha explained soup proportions and pointed out century eggs, I found them, received gloves from Tasha, and agreed to assist. Gloves and ingredient locations were confirmed for soup prep.

I prepared and pressed flowers using gloves. I put on white gloves, checked the flower-pressing board and petals, placed petals and crouched to press, and coordinated technique and equipment with Tasha and Katrina. Flower pressing was initiated and methods tested.

I assisted Alice with a can and retrieved cables/devices. I opened a can for Alice, placed trash on the table, returned to my bedroom to inspect and pick up data cables and a power bank, and noted the bowl and unwashed knife locations per Alice. I provided kitchen help and located small electronics.

I inspected the second-floor living room, restored a computer, and worked. I walked upstairs, observed devices, unplugged and replugged a power cord to the computer, turned it on, smoothed cables, returned downstairs, set seating, sat at the computer, operated software and the phone, and then plugged my phone in to charge. The computer was powered and I proceeded to work while charging my phone.

I continue multitasking between devices. I work on the primary computer while referencing my phone and additional windows, repeatedly looking at phone screens, opening other windows, and keeping my hand on the computer. Ongoing simultaneous computer and phone operation is maintained.

I address the group about a light. I turn to everyone and exchange brief remarks: Alice says "Give me," I reply "Hmm" then "Okay," Lucia reports "I suddenly can't find the light in my room," I say "Haha," and Alice responds "That's okay." The exchange records Alice’s request, my confirmations, Lucia’s report about the light, and brief group responses.

I resume device checks and video review and state status. I check my phone for errors, watch one recorded video, open other recorded videos while operating the computer, and explicitly say "I'm still checking" to indicate the inspection continues. Video and error checks remain ongoing.

I inspect files on the computer and make a physical adjustment. I operate the computer to open additional videos and inspect file contents, and I adjust my glasses during this content review. The activity confirms continued computer-based review with a brief physical adjustment.

I make a brief verbal address and use the phone then set it aside. I say "Hey," pinch my nose, turn on the phone, open WeChat, then power the phone down and place it on the sofa. A short address and a quick WeChat check are completed and the phone is set aside.

I survey the living area and prepare to power a chair computer, then move to and use a second machine. I stand, scan the living room, walk into the kitchen and back, check devices while glancing at the ceiling camera, crouch to inspect the chair computer and indicate I will turn it on, then walk to the other computer, pull out the chair, and begin operating it. I transition from inspection to active use of the secondary computer.

Visitors report the downstairs food and egg success; group plans cleanup. Visitors announce the food I made downstairs is a success; Katrina reports she successfully cracked an egg after previous failures and others confirm; later I crack an egg successfully while the group notes a small item "doesn't work" and "needs a wipe," moving toward cleaning. The meal outcome is confirmed and the group proceeds to minor fixes and cleanup.

A time check and egg-white request occur. Lucia asks the time and whether I can check possible egg-white on her hand; numeric exchanges occur (Katrina and Lucia give numbers, Lucia corrects and apologizes), and the request to check Lucia’s hand is raised. The time is corrected and Lucia’s request is noted.

I shift focus to another video and move to view the sofa computer. I open another video on the computer, stand, scan the living room, walk to the sofa, crouch down, and look at the computer on the sofa. I transition from screen viewing to physically approaching the sofa device for continued review.
